entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00062
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00062; the file is 373,582 bytes.

The XML has two DIV1 divisions, forty-five DIV2 selections, eight nested DIV3 divisions, 7,393 L elements in 111 LG elements, three prose P elements, 98 notes, and seventy-seven PB elements. Printed p. 266 is represented by two PB milestones, so PB count is not physical page count.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, 'Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon,' Archiv fuer das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 57 (31. Jahrgang, 1877): 241-316.
bibliographic.container_title: Archiv fuer das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen
bibliographic.title: Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon

entities.label: Carl Horstmann's 1877 selection from the Vernon Homily Collection
notes: This is the immediate printed source represented by CME00062.

The article prints forty-five selected narratives in source order except that items 39 and 40 reverse their Vernon order; the addendum on p. 316 prints thirty-four lines from a separate location in the Cana homily.
Horstmann selects narratives from their homiletic settings and sometimes abridges within a selection; item 29c explicitly omits an introductory passage of about one hundred lines.

rights.basis: Published in 1877 and far outside modern copyright terms; this does not grant rights in later scans or encodings.
rights.component: 1877 article, editorial matter, and printed Middle English text
rights.jurisdiction: United States and ordinary copyright jurisdictions

entities.description: A monumental late-fourteenth-century English parchment codex whose current fols. 167r-318r contain the Northern Homily Cycle and associated texts. Horstmann's selected passages occur discontinuously from current fol. 167r through fol. 215v, with the addendum at fol. 176r.

holding.notes: The origin and patronage remain debated; the current record tentatively gives England, West Midlands.

entities.label: Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS. Eng. poet. a. 1 (Vernon Manuscript)

physicalDescription.extent: viii + 342 + i leaves, incomplete; originally viii + 413, with 71 leaves thought lost
physicalDescription.format: Large codex, normally quires of eight; nineteenth-century crushed russet leather over bevelled oak boards
notes: The current digital sequence jumps from fol. 126v to fol. 167r because forty-one leaves are missing at that point.
The complete codex contains more than 350 religious and didactic items, so the selected passages are only a small, discontinuous portion of the whole physical object.

rights.basis: The manuscript and its medieval textual contents predate copyright by centuries; this status is separate from modern photography and catalog text.
rights.component: Medieval manuscript text and underlying historical work

access.accessMethod: Digital Bodleian viewer or IIIF manifest
access.entityLabel: Complete Digital Bodleian Vernon Manuscript object
access.format: 710 color IIIF canvases and complete local provider-object bundle

localCopies.label: Complete Bodleian Vernon Manuscript IIIF bundle
notes: Exact hardlink reuse of the existing complete CME00052 bundle; checksum and inode identity verified.
The bundle includes all 710 published canvases, covers, flyleaves, blanks, and spine, with adjacent texts retained. It has no dedicated edge views; effective profile is 1600 pixels wide.

Supporting comparison outside work_portion: the item 14 footnote's analogous Theodora narrative maps under the current Digital Bodleian structure to fols. 93r-v, canvases 171-172; it is not part of the forty-five-item selection.

workPortion.label: All Vernon passages selected in CME00062, mapped within the complete manuscript
locators: Item 1: current fols. 167r-v, canvases 221-222 (Horstmann legacy fol. 126)
Placidas, printed as a second item 15: fols. 181r-183v, canvases 249-254
Item 29a-b: fols. 196r-197r, canvases 279-281; item 29c: fols. 197r-199r, canvases 281-285
Item 40: fols. 210v-211r, canvases 308-309 (manuscript order precedes item 39)
notes: These locators are intentionally non-contiguous and include the addendum separately; they do not imply that intervening leaves belong to the selected work.

bibliographic.citation: John Small, ed., English Metrical Homilies from Manuscripts of the Fourteenth Century (Edinburgh: William Paterson, 1862).
bibliographic.title: English Metrical Homilies from Manuscripts of the Fourteenth Century

entities.label: John Small's English Metrical Homilies (1862)
notes: Small prints the fragmentary Royal College of Physicians manuscript through the Purification and supplies gaps from CUL MS Gg.5.31 and Bodleian MS. Ashmole 42.
Horstmann states that Vernon follows Small's Edinburgh witness through item 12, except that item 4 is absent there, and explicitly cites variant readings from Small.

rights.basis: Published in 1862 and outside modern copyright terms.
rights.component: 1862 edition and editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States and ordinary copyright jurisdictions

entities.description: An early-fourteenth-century composite parchment fragment. The Northern Homily Cycle occupies fols. 16r-36v between Cursor Mundi fragments and ends early in the second Purification exemplum; three leaves are missing after fol. 24.

holding.notes: Small calls it a manuscript of Cursor Mundi belonging to the Royal College of Physicians; the College's public catalog page warns that not all holdings are listed.
holding.shelfmark: Exact current call number not established

entities.label: Edinburgh, Royal College of Physicians, Cursor Mundi manuscript with Northern Homily Cycle fragment

physicalDescription.extent: 50 leaves total; Northern Homily Cycle at fols. 16r-36v, with three leaves missing after fol. 24
physicalDescription.format: Thin quarto; two columns; generally 43 or 45 lines
notes: Bequeathed to the College in 1741 by Dr John Drummond; generally treated as the earliest and least expanded surviving witness of the cycle.

rights.basis: The underlying medieval text is public domain; this does not determine rights in any newly supplied photographs.

next_steps: Email library@rcpe.ac.uk with Small's 1862 description and request all 50 leaves plus covers, binding, spine, flyleaves, blanks, mounts, and edges if offered; ask separately for fols. 16r-36v mapping, price, resolution, and publication/commercial-use terms.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current call number of Small's Edinburgh Cursor Mundi/Northern Homily Cycle manuscript, and can RCPE supply a complete physical-object color facsimile under reusable terms?

next_steps: Consult the full Vernon catalog bibliography or ask Bodleian manuscripts staff for the foliation history; retain both locators and do not replace current fol. 167r with legacy fol. 126.
openQuestions.question: Can a Bodleian or scholarly concordance explicitly confirm that Horstmann's first legacy fol. 126 corresponds to current Vernon fol. 167r after the forty-one-leaf lacuna?

next_steps: Request current foliations and contents concordances from CUL, Bodleian, and BL before adding item-level mappings; never infer loci from Horstmann's broad witness list.
openQuestions.question: What exact current folios contain the corresponding selected passages in Dd.1.1, Gg.5.31, Ashmole 42, and the item 29 comparison in Harley 4196?

next_steps: Search Horstmann's methodological prefaces and compare each queried italic span with the mapped Vernon canvas; record exceptions if found.
openQuestions.question: Did Horstmann state anywhere outside this article that every italic letter in the transcription represents an expanded manuscript abbreviation?

next_steps: Preserve the printed duplicate 15 in source-facing data and use sequence position 16 only as an explicit normalized convenience unless contemporary errata or later scholarship resolves it.
openQuestions.question: Is the second printed number 15 for Placidas a typographical error for 16, or an intentional numbering artifact?

relations.subjectLabel: Carl Horstmann's 1877 selection from the Vernon Homily Collection

reviewNotes: The Northern Homily Cycle, the Vernon codex, Horstmann's selected edition, Small's comparison edition, the U-M encoding, and digital facsimiles are modeled as distinct layers.

Complete local bundles retain every provider-published image, including material outside the corpus selection. work_portion locators associate the selected passages without redefining either bundle as a cropped facsimile.
The inherited NLS Adv. MS. 19.3.1 bundle was checked and excluded: it is the Heege Manuscript, not the Edinburgh Royal College of Physicians witness edited by Small.
Horstmann's closing dateline, Sagan i. J. 1876, is editorial place/date evidence; the volume title page establishes publication at Braunschweig in 1877.

sourceLinks.format: No current public whole-object image access; do not substitute a selected fol. 64 image for a complete manuscript surrogate

lineage.summary: The repository XML represents University of Michigan CME item CME00062, a complete encoding of Carl Horstmann's 1877 article in Archiv fuer das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 57, pp. 241-316. Horstmann extracts forty-five Middle English verse narratives and a short addendum from the Northern Homily Cycle and associated material in the Vernon Manuscript, while consulting John Small's 1862 edition and several other witnesses. The complete 500-canvas BSB journal object and complete 710-canvas Digital Bodleian Vernon object are cached. Every selected Vernon passage is mapped within that full manuscript bundle; the mapping is necessarily non-contiguous, and the addendum is separately located in the Cana homily. Public whole-object images were not found for the named comparison manuscripts on 2026-07-12, so request routes and bounded uncertainties are recorded instead of selected-page downloads.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous Northern Homily Cycle poet
catalogSummary.date: Original Northern Homily Cycle probably composed around 1300 or in the early fourteenth century; Vernon expanded recension copied 1381-1400, commonly c. 1390; edited selection published 1877
catalogSummary.region: Original cycle associated with northern England, perhaps Yorkshire; Vernon copy associated tentatively with the West Midlands; Horstmann's editorial dateline is Sagan (Zagan), 1876
catalogSummary.title: Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon

catalogingSubject.label: Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The intellectual and editorial unit published by Carl Horstmann in Archiv 57 (1877), pp. 241-316, and represented as CME00062: forty-five selected Middle English verse narratives from the Northern Homily Cycle and associated material in the Vernon Manuscript, German and Latin editorial headings and notes, and a thirty-four-line addendum drawn from a separate place in the Cana homily. It is not the complete Northern Homily Cycle, not the whole Vernon Manuscript, and not a single medieval work.

contentParts.label: 1. Conversion of Mary Magdalene
contentParts.summary: An eighty-two-line conversion narrative headed De conversione Marie Magdalene.

contentParts.label: 2. A dead monk reveals his state
contentParts.summary: A ninety-line monastic otherworld exemplum.

contentParts.label: 3. Death of John the Baptist
contentParts.summary: An eighty-five-line narrative of John's death, presented in place of a tale.

contentParts.label: 4. The impoverished knight devoted to the Virgin
contentParts.summary: A 122-line Marian exemplum absent from Small's principal Edinburgh witness.

contentParts.label: 5. The pilgrim to St James
contentParts.summary: A 138-line penitential miracle concerning a pilgrim who mutilates himself.

contentParts.label: 6. Three desert-saint exempla
contentParts.summary: A 126-line group: the devil appears to St Martin; humility escapes the devil's wiles; and Amon trusts wholly in God.

contentParts.label: 7. The archbishop and the nun
contentParts.summary: A 382-line episcopal and monastic exemplum with several Small variants noted by Horstmann.

contentParts.label: 8. The bishop misjudges St John's child
contentParts.summary: A 108-line narrative concerning St John and a boy.

contentParts.label: 9. Spiritual things should not be sold
contentParts.summary: A sixty-line tale of Gehazi and Naaman against simony.

contentParts.label: 10. The knight's penance among worms
contentParts.summary: A 142-line penitential exemplum.

contentParts.label: 11. Macarius sees the devil's medicines
contentParts.summary: A one-hundred-line desert-father exemplum.

contentParts.label: 12. Two miracles of the Virgin
contentParts.summary: A 260-line group: the Virgin gives a good wife a candle, and delivers a pregnant abbess.

contentParts.label: 13. The hermit called but not chosen
contentParts.summary: A forty-six-line eremitic exemplum.

contentParts.label: 14. St Marina is defamed
contentParts.summary: A 228-line Marina legend; Horstmann cites a different version in Harley 2253 fol. 64 and an analogous Theodora tale in Vernon under legacy fol. 92.

contentParts.label: 15. A Pater Noster for St Bernard's palfrey
contentParts.summary: A fifty-two-line St Bernard exemplum.

contentParts.label: 15 [sequence 16]. The story of Placidas
notes: Do not silently normalize the printed heading to 16 in source-facing displays.
contentParts.summary: A 1,060-line legend of Eustace/Placidas. Horstmann prints a second number 15 and then continues with 17; sequence 16 is supplied only for navigation.

contentParts.label: 17. A hermit drives another to despair
contentParts.summary: A 164-line eremitic narrative of despair and judgment.

contentParts.label: 18. Stones answer St Bede
contentParts.summary: A forty-line miracle narrative associated with Bede.

contentParts.label: 19. The wife and her husband's shirt
contentParts.summary: A seventy-two-line memorial and marital exemplum.

contentParts.label: 20. Of St Martin
contentParts.summary: A thirty-six-line St Martin narrative.

contentParts.label: 21. The devil tempts sleepers during preaching
contentParts.summary: A twenty-six-line exemplum about attention to God's word.

contentParts.label: 22. The devil would have encumbered St Edmund
contentParts.summary: A sixteen-line St Edmund exemplum.

contentParts.label: 23. The founder of Clairvaux saved by a leper
contentParts.summary: A sixty-six-line charitable exemplum associated with the founder of Clairvaux.

contentParts.label: 24. The monk who desired to see the least bliss of Paradise
contentParts.summary: A ninety-eight-line vision or otherworld exemplum.

contentParts.label: 25. Carpus
contentParts.summary: A sixty-six-line Carpus exemplum.

contentParts.label: 26. The obedient servant
contentParts.summary: A twenty-six-line obedience exemplum without a descriptive printed heading.

contentParts.label: 27. Conversion of Thais
contentParts.summary: A fifty-four-line conversion legend of Thais the courtesan.

contentParts.label: 28. The king frightens his brother with minstrelsy
contentParts.summary: A fifty-four-line royal exemplum that Horstmann parallels with Barlaam and Josaphat.

contentParts.label: 29. Feast of Corpus Christi and Eucharistic miracles

locators.value: parts a-b fols. 196r-197r, canvases 279-281; part c fols. 197r-199r, canvases 281-285

notes: The printed heading says seven miracles, while the XML exposes labeled subitems A-F after the introductory parts; do not infer a complete autonomous Seven Miracles text from this extract.
contentParts.summary: A 724-line multi-part selection: two Eucharistic explanations or narratives followed by selected Septem Miracula. Horstmann omits about one hundred introductory lines before the miracles; the current Vernon structure identifies the latter passage as an extract from Robert of Brunne's Handlyng Synne.

contentParts.label: 30. The angel and the hermit who judged contrary odors
contentParts.summary: A forty-nine-line angelic-hermit exemplum.

contentParts.label: 31. One judges another for breaking his fast
contentParts.summary: A forty-eight-line warning against harsh judgment.

contentParts.label: 32. St Oswald the king
contentParts.summary: A 142-line royal saint narrative.

contentParts.label: 33. The story of Theophilus
contentParts.summary: A 686-line Theophilus legend concerning apostasy, repentance, and Marian intercession.

contentParts.label: 34. The priest and the maiden
contentParts.summary: A 112-line clerical sexual-sin and repentance exemplum.

contentParts.label: 35. The man who withheld alms to gather money
contentParts.summary: A ninety-eight-line charity and avarice exemplum.

contentParts.label: 36. The holy monk with a wicked brother
contentParts.summary: A 109-line fraternal and monastic salvation exemplum.

contentParts.label: 37. Conversion of Pelagia
contentParts.summary: A 480-line conversion legend of Pelagia.

contentParts.label: 38. The knight who forgave his father's killer
contentParts.summary: An eighty-six-line forgiveness exemplum.

contentParts.label: 39. Gregory prays for a damned man
contentParts.summary: A forty-line Gregory and Trajan intercession narrative. Horstmann places it before item 40, although the Vernon sequence has item 40 first.

contentParts.label: 40. Alexander encloses the Jews within the Caspian mountains
contentParts.summary: A seventy-eight-line Alexander narrative. Its Vernon passage begins before item 39 in manuscript order.

contentParts.label: 41. The three aunts of St Gregory
contentParts.summary: A 118-line narrative of Gregory's three aunts.

contentParts.label: 42. The nun who feigned herself foul
contentParts.summary: A 156-line humility and monastic reputation exemplum.

contentParts.label: 43. The backbiting monk
contentParts.summary: A forty-six-line warning against slander.

contentParts.label: 44. Amon and Mordecai
contentParts.summary: A 238-line Esther narrative, headed in Middle English with Amon and Mordecai.

contentParts.label: 45. Piers the Toller and the white loaf
contentParts.summary: A 350-line charity and salvation narrative. Horstmann reports that Harley 4196 places the parallel at fol. 54 in another homiletic position and notes a variant reading.

contentParts.label: Zusatz: Christ turns water into wine
notes: Its separate source locus is why it is mapped independently in the complete Vernon bundle.
contentParts.summary: An unnumbered thirty-four-line addendum printed from the middle of the Cana homily after an explanation of water as sin and wine as grace. It is not contiguous with item 45 in the manuscript.

assertion.notes: The earliest surviving witness is early fourteenth century; the narrower Carver hypothesis is useful but not definitive.

dateStatements.display: Probably around 1300 or in the early fourteenth century; a proposed 1295-1306 range is not universally accepted

scope.description: Underlying original Northern Homily Cycle material represented in the editorial unit

assertion.notes: Revision history is not automatically identical with the copying date of this surviving codex.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth century, before or during production of the Vernon Manuscript

scope.description: Expanded Vernon recension and associated selected material represented in the editorial unit

dateStatements.display: 1381-1400 in the Vernon Manuscript, commonly dated c. 1390
notes: This is attestation of these Vernon forms, not first attestation of every narrative motif.

scope.description: The exact Vernon readings transcribed by Horstmann within the editorial unit

assertion.notes: This is not the medieval composition date and not the journal publication year.

scope.description: Horstmann's signed editorial completion dateline

notes: The volume title page, not the article's 1876 dateline, controls publication date.

extent.description: Forty-five printed numbered selections (with number 15 duplicated and 16 absent) plus one unnumbered thirty-four-line addendum. The XML contains 7,359 lines in the numbered selections and 34 in the addendum, for 7,393 L elements total, grouped in 111 LG elements. Three prose P elements belong to editorial framing, not prose-only medieval items.

formStatements.description: The cataloged article combines 7,393 lines of Middle English verse with German prose framing, headings, and notes.
Rhyming Middle English verse, predominantly octosyllabic in the underlying cycle.

scope.description: Forty-five medieval selections and the addendum
Underlying Northern Homily Cycle contexts represented by the selection
Most separately selected narratives are exempla within the cataloged editorial unit
Named saints and saintly protagonists
Marian, Eucharistic, penitential, and intercessory miracle narratives represented across the selection

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The original cycle is northern; the Vernon recension reflects later Midland transmission.

scope.description: Middle English is the primary content language of all forty-five selections and the verse addendum
Article title, editorial notes, some headings, and addendum framing
Latin occurs in editorial headings, Gospel cues, and embedded formulae throughout the unit

notes: The content part list is complete at the printed selection level: forty-five numbered items plus the addendum. Nested sub-exempla inside items 6, 12, and 29 are summarized but not falsely counted as additional top-level numbered items.
The overall form is mixed because the cataloged editorial unit includes prose framing; every medieval content part listed here is verse.
All manuscript page references use current Vernon foliation unless explicitly labeled Horstmann legacy foliation.
The complete Vernon facsimile is locally cached but subject to CC BY-NC 4.0; local availability is not publication permission.

next_steps: Review post-2008 scholarship on the Carver 1295-1306 proposal and record competing ranges with separate evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can the original Northern Homily Cycle's composition date be narrowed beyond around 1300 or the early fourteenth century without adopting a disputed hypothesis as fact?

next_steps: Consult Vernon codicological literature or ask Bodleian staff; retain both labels in the meantime.
openQuestions.question: Can an authoritative foliation concordance confirm Horstmann's legacy fol. 126 as current Vernon fol. 167r after the forty-one-leaf lacuna?

next_steps: Look for contemporary errata or later discussion; retain printed 15 and separately expose normalized sequence 16.
openQuestions.question: Was Placidas deliberately printed as a second item 15, or is it an uncorrected error for 16?

next_steps: Compare the full Vernon passage with a critical Handlyng Synne edition and the official Bodleian range hierarchy before assigning component authorship or autonomous titles.
openQuestions.question: How should item 29's Corpus Christi material, omitted introduction, and selected Handlyng Synne miracles be divided into stable component works?

next_steps: Compare queried spans against the mapped complete Vernon facsimile and seek an explicit Horstmann editorial statement; do not mechanically reverse italics into manuscript marks.
openQuestions.question: Do all 589 italic spans in the CME encoding represent Horstmann's manuscript-abbreviation expansions, or do some serve another editorial function?

summaries.value: Horstmann's article extracts forty-five verse narratives from the Northern Homily Cycle and associated material in the Vernon Manuscript. The stories range across conversion, penance, saints, monastic life, Marian aid, Eucharistic miracles, judgment, forgiveness, and salvation. German editorial notes compare the opening section with John Small's edition of the earliest surviving witness and distinguish the substantially revised Harley-Cotton recension. A final addendum prints thirty-four lines from a separate point in the Cana homily.

The forty-five numbered selections include Marian and penitential exempla; three short desert-saint tales in item 6; two Marian tales in item 12; the Eustace/Placidas legend; conversion legends of Thais and Pelagia; a multi-part Corpus Christi and Eucharistic miracle sequence in item 29; Theophilus; Gregory and Trajan; Esther; and Piers the Toller. The source prints two successive items as number 15 and then continues at 17. The addendum is unnumbered.

Catalog dates and places separate the original cycle, the Vernon expanded recension and witness, Horstmann's 1876 dateline, and 1877 publication. The selected tales are not collapsed into one medieval composition, and manuscript folios are not treated as article page numbers.

titles.supplied_by: Horstmann's printed article title, omitting the responsibility phrase

notes: The final phrase is a statement of editorial responsibility, not medieval authorship.
titles.value: Die Evangelien-Geschichten der Homiliensammlung des Ms. Vernon, ausgezogen von Dr. Carl Horstmann

notes: Stories is intentionally broad: the selection contains exempla, saints' legends, miracle narratives, and other embedded religious tales.

The stories range across conversion, penance, saints, monastic life, Marian aid, Eucharistic miracles, judgment, forgiveness, and salvation. German editorial notes compare the opening section with John Small's edition of the earliest surviving witness and distinguish the substantially revised Harley-Cotton recension.
